Solution Design Document

CountR GmbH

▪ v1 Oct 20, 2025 Guido Roepke (groepke@paypal.com)

Introduction

CountR GmbH is a global leader in the development of advanced payment systems and automated cash-handling solutions. From multifunctional terminals to customized software, they serve a wide range of industries, including casinos, retail and entertainment.

This Solution Design Document outlines a collaboration between PayPal and CountR. Now, 8.000 terminals in more than 40 countries are located, mainly in casinos and US gas stations. A user can top up its gaming account on a machine with cash and receive a voucher or card for playing in the casino. After gaming, the remaining balance can be paid out in cash at the machine. CountR are planning to offer PayPal and Venmo as additional payment methods. Their focus is on US, but a pilot might be run in DE due to the proximity of casinos to the headquarters of PayPal and CountR.

The pay-in use case will be the following: user presses “Top up with PayPal” on the screen of the terminal, gets a QR code displayed and scans the code with its smartphone. A landing page will be

1. OAuth

1.1. Basics

The PayPal APIs are available in two environments: the Live (or Production) environment and the Sandbox environment.

The Sandbox environment is a testing environment that is designed to be a mirror of the PayPal Live environment. Any actions taken in the Sandbox environment — such as processing a payment — have no "real world" consequences outside of the Sandbox. Use the Sandbox environment for testing purposes. Once your integration is complete and you are ready to begin processing payments, move to the Live environment for production processing.

In this section, we'll describe the various PayPal accounts that you need to create, as well as any other PayPal accounts that may come into play during the course of your integration with PayPal.

1.1.1. Account Setup

1.1.1.1. API Caller Account

The purpose of the API caller account is to own the API credentials that will be used to make all API calls associated with your integration. It must be a PayPal Business account. While you may be able to reuse an existing PayPal account (check with PayPal to be sure), PayPal recommends that you set up a new account to be your API caller account for this integration. CountR GmbH is responsible for creating and maintaining this account.

Once you've created this account, please provide the email address of the account to PayPal so that PayPal can perform its due diligence and vetting processes. Once you've completed your integration and all necessary approvals have been obtained, PayPal will configure this account to be able to use all of the relevant features described in this document.

1.1.1.2. Receiver Accounts

Each seller who will accept payments on your platform will need their own PayPal account. These accounts are known as "receiver accounts", "recipient accounts", "merchant accounts", or "seller accounts". Sellers can connect an existing PayPal account to CountR GmbH's platform; however, sellers who do not have a PayPal account do not need to create one before attempting to connect a PayPal account to your platform — they can create one during the connection process.

1.1.2. Environments

There are separate endpoints for the Sandbox and Live environments, as shown below:

Sandbox Live

The PayPal Developer Portal can help you create and manage your accounts in the Sandbox environment. You can access the PayPal Developer Portal by going to https://developer.paypal.com/. Once you've created your Sandbox accounts, you can access them by going to https://www.sandbox.paypal.com.

1.1.3. Logging, Outages and Monitoring

1.1.3.1. Logging

PayPal strongly recommends that you log all of the API calls that you make to PayPal for debugging purposes. Logging should include the HTTP request and response (including HTTP headers for both the request and response). Credentials and personally identifiable information should be masked.

All of PayPal's REST API responses contain a PayPal-Debug-Id HTTP header that uniquely identifies each request. Include this value in your logging along with the timestamp of the API call, and provide both to PayPal technical support when requesting assistance.

1.1.3.2. Log Persistence

```
API Calls https://api-m.sandbox.paypal.com https://api-m.paypal.com
```

```
Web Flows https://www.sandbox.paypal.com https://www.paypal.com
```

In general, for any Live interface or service available on host.paypal.com, there will be a corresponding Sandbox interface or service available on host.sandbox.paypal.com.

Note that the Sandbox and Live environments are not directly connected. Some things that exist in the Sandbox environment — including accounts, credentials, etc. — do not necessarily exist in the Live environment, and vice-versa. When switching between the Live and Sandbox environments, be sure to use the values that correspond to the correct environment for the following:

• Hostname • Login credentials (email address, password, etc.) • API credentials (client ID, secret, etc.)

o Partner ID • Merchant account IDs

o BN code

When testing in the Sandbox, you will need at least one receiver account. PayPal recommends that you have at least one buyer account as well, although this is not strictly required.

PayPal recommends that you keep logs of unsuccessful API calls for a period of at least two weeks. Logs of successful calls may be purged after a shorter amount of time. Note that PayPal purges most of their logs after two weeks and may not be able to assist you in diagnosing an error if the error occurred more than two weeks ago.

1.1.3.3. Outages

• Register your project; i.e. "create a REST API app" • Obtain API credentials and manage them • Create Sandbox accounts • Register webhooks • View webhooks sent to you

For more information, please review the official Get Started documentation at https://developer.paypal.com/docs/multiparty/get-started/.

This workspace is designed specifically for our partners to explore, test, and integrate PayPal capabilities using Postman.

PayPal strongly recommends that you subscribe to PayPal's outage notifications, via https://www.paypal-status.com/. This will help keep you informed about upcoming maintenance and irregular outages.

Note that PayPal updates their systems on a regular basis. This will mean that PayPal will shutdown, update and boot individual server cluster nodes at times. Typically this will be done in a staggered fashion so that business is not affected.

1.1.3.4. Monitoring

Partners are advised to monitor PayPal API traffic and to look for failed API calls or unexpected times of silence — such as an hour of no successful API calls to PayPal — which might indicate a more systematic issue.

1.1.4. Developer Dashboard

In order to register your project at PayPal and receive API credentials, please go to https://developer.paypal.com.

Note: When creating or configuring your Live project, be sure to log in using the PayPal account that you've designated as the API caller account. If you're creating or configuring a Sandbox project, you can log in using any PayPal account.

On the Developer Dashboard you can:

1.1.6. Negative Testing

The PayPal Sandbox environment allows you to deliberately return API call errors for you to verify your own correct handling of them, e.g. when trying to refund a payment with a faulty transaction ID. For more information, please see the Negative Test documentation at https://developer.paypal.com/docs/api/nt-rest.

Note: Without permissions from the other PayPal account, you may still be able to perform some actions on the other account's behalf; however, you will be limited in which actions you can take.

1.1.7. Authentication Basics

1.1.7.1. Client ID and Secret

These are the credentials associated with a PayPal REST API app and are used to retrieve API access tokens. You can retrieve the Client ID and Secret for your app and environment from the Developer Dashboard.

Your Client ID is a public credential and can be safely exposed externally. The Secret, on the other hand, must be handled securely and only shared with people or systems on a need-to-know basis.

Note: If you reasonably believe that an unauthorized person may have obtained access to your Secret, you must immediately request a new Secret from the Developer Dashboard. PayPal will create a second Secret, which you can begin to use immediately. Your previous Secret will remain active as well, giving you time to rotate your Secret without interrupting service to your users. (You may have a maximum of two Secrets on any given REST API app at any one time.) Once you have rotated your Secret, you must immediately delete the previous Secret.

1.1.7.2. First-Party vs. Third-Party

[Document: PayPal Docs | Chapter: Chapter 1 | Section: 1. OAuth | Page: 10-11]

PayPal uses an authentication and authorization model that distinguishes between API calls made on ones own behalf (e.g., on behalf of the PayPal account that was used to create the API credentials) and API calls that are made on behalf of another PayPal account. When an API call is made on your own behalf, it is known as a "first-party" call; when an API call is made on behalf of another PayPal account, it is known as a "third-party" call.

In general, you must have consent (or "permissions") from the other PayPal account before you can make API calls on their behalf. PayPal offers different mechanisms for obtaining this consent, which will vary depending on your use case and the API stack your integration will use. These mechanisms are detailed later in this document. Permissions are granular — meaning that you must request permission to access to specific pieces of information and/or permission to perform specific actions from the other user. (For example, the ability to process payments and the ability to process refunds are separate permissions.) As a best practice, you should not request more permissions than your integration requires.

A partner, in the context of PayPal payments, is to be understood as a PayPal account owner who will orchestrate funds movement on behalf of a seller that will receive money from their customers, as shown below:

PayPal requires all partners to provide a BN code with all of their API calls. The BN code is a character string that uniquely identifies your platform and your integration, and allows PayPal to properly attribute the payments that you facilitate to you. PayPal will

The following are examples of first-party API calls that you might make as part of your integration:

• Obtaining an access token • Initiating ISU onboarding

The following are examples of third-party API calls that you might make as part of your integration:

• Creating and executing a payment on behalf of a seller • Processing a refund on behalf of a seller • Obtaining information about the seller • Retrieving the transaction history of a seller • Retrieving information on a seller's dispute or responding to the dispute on the seller's behalf

1.1.7.2.1. Delegated First-Party

When making third-party API calls, you must generally store information about the other PayPal account in your system — such as a merchant ID or a refresh token — and use that information to make third-party calls on behalf of the other account. This may present a challenge to cart software providers — who do not typically host their software on systems that they own or control — as it will require the cart software provider to either — depending on the solution integrated — (a) provide services that will make API calls on behalf of the seller's instance of the cart software, or (b) provide a new access token to the seller's instance of the cart software whenever the previous one has expired.

To simplify this problem, PayPal offers the delegated first-party authentication pattern. In this pattern, the seller grants a one-time permission to you to retrieve their API credentials from PayPal. This approach allows you to communicate the credentials directly to the seller's instance of your software (recommended). The seller can also create their own REST API app from the Developer Dashboard and copy their Client ID and Secret to your application.

1.1.8. Commercial Tracking Through BN Codes

1.1.9. Certification of Integration

Certification of CountR GmbH's integration is required after development is complete and before go-live. This document, and optionally the Certification Checklist, will serve as the guidelines for certification. Since certification will be a joint effort from CountR GmbH and PayPal, both parties will allocate appropriate resources.

Throughout the integration process, CountR GmbH should update the PayPal team of their progress and the user experience of the integration. This will ensure the appropriate reviews are conducted timely.

1.2. Access Token

Use OAuth Access Token to authenticate API calls relating to your account itself — not with regards to another PayPal account, e.g. a specific seller or customer. Typical lifetime of an Access Token is 9 hours after which a fresh one has to be obtained.

Attempts to perform API calls with an invalid or expired token will fail and result in a HTTP 401 Unauthorized status. The implementation must be able to handle these situations appropriately.

Note further that OAuth Access Tokens need to be cached during their lifetime so that no redundant API calls are made, i.e. to obtain an Access Token although it is still valid.

2. Onboarding

2.1. Integrated Sign-Up seller onboarding

Integrated Sign-Up is a powerful onboarding tool that helps your sellers get their PayPal account set up to take payments on your platform.

It can handle a wide variety of scenarios — such as sellers who are new to PayPal, sellers who have an existing PayPal Personal or PayPal Premier account, and sellers who already have a PayPal Business account.

Integrated Sign-Up takes your sellers through all of the steps necessary to set up a new account (or upgrade an existing account), provision it with the products and capabilities needed to be able to accept payments on your platform, and grant the proper set of API permissions to your platform.

NOTE: Sellers must have a PayPal business account in order to receive payments. Don't worry, sellers with personal or premier accounts will be able to upgrade for free within the Integrated Sign-Up flow.

2.1.1. How it works

CountR GmbH use the following steps to complete seller onboarding:

1. Seller clicks a link or button on CountR GmbH's platform indicating

"Create/Connect a PayPal account". 2. CountR GmbH makes a Create Partner Referral API call that contains

information about the products and capabilities the seller is to sign up for, the API permissions CountR GmbH is seeking from the seller, and — optionally — the seller's information. 3. CountR GmbH redirects the seller to the approval URL returned by PayPal. 4. Once sellers have completed the Integrated Sign-Up flow, they are returned to

CountR GmbH website. CountR GmbH must fetch the seller PayPal ID from of the followings:

o 4a. The parameters appended to the return URL. o 4b. The Get seller PayPal id API response. o 4c. The MERCHANT.ONBOARDING.COMPLETED webhook. 5. Finally, thanks to the seller PayPal id, CountR GmbH calls the show seller status

API in order to display the onboarding status back to the seller.

2.1.3. Email Confirmation in Sandbox

When you create a test account through the PayPal Developer Dashboard, PayPal automatically marks the email address on the test account as "confirmed". However, when an account is created through the Integrated Sign-Up web flow, the email address on the test account must be confirmed manually. Since the PayPal Sandbox does not send real emails, you will need to take some extra steps to confirm the email address on the account.

3.4. Requirement to Respond

PayPal has logic in place to retry the delivery of a webhook event when in the event of a failure. This logic ensures that transient errors or network outages do not cause a webhook to be lost. When sending a webhook event, PayPal uses the HTTP response code to determine whether the webhook event was delivered successfully. If PayPal receives an HTTP 200 OK response (or another HTTP 2XX-series response), PayPal considers the webhook event to be delivered. Any other response — or the lack of a response — is considered a failure. If a failure occurs, PayPal will make several more attempts to deliver the webhook event; these attempts will be spread out at increasing intervals over the course of a few days. If PayPal continually receives failures from your webhook, PayPal may consider the webhook to be permanently down and stop sending webhook events to it. Therefore, to ensure that webhook events continue to be delivered to you, you must ensure that your webhook responds with an HTTP 200 OK response (or other HTTP 2XX-series response) upon successfully receiving a webhook event.

Further details are available at https://developer.paypal.com/api/rest/webhooks/.

5.2. PayPal Complete Payments (PPCP)

5.2.1. Features

The PayPal Complete Payments (PPCP) is PayPal's newest payment processing API suite. The PPCP API suite encompasses the following payment methods:

• PayPal (branded wallet experience) • Venmo

5.2.2. Get started with PayPal REST APIs

PayPal integrations use client ID and client secret to authenticate API calls:

• A client ID identifies an app. You only need a client ID to get a PayPal payment button and standard credit and debit card fields.

• A client secret authenticates a client ID. To call PayPal APIs, you'll exchange your client ID and client secret for an access token. Keep this secret safe.

Here's how to get your client ID and client secret:

1. Select Log in to Dashboard and log in or sign up. 2. Select Apps & Credentials.  3. New accounts come with a Default Application in the REST API apps section.

To create a new project, select Create App. 4. Copy the client ID and client secret for your app.

For more details, please refer to the official documentation at:  https://developer.paypal.com/api/rest/.

5.2.3. Frontend and Backend

As opposed to only offering a backend API as PayPal did in previous API suites, e.g. the Express Checkout / Classic API, PPCP offers both a backend API and a frontend library, called JavaScript SDK (JS SDK).

A PPCP integration will thus always involve two items:

• Backend API, called Orders v2 • Frontend SDK, called JavaScript SDK (JS SDK)

6. Operational and Servicing

6.1. Disputes API

6.1.1. Workflow

Occasionally, something goes wrong with an order. A dispute occurs when a customer asks his or her bank or credit card company to reverse a charge or opens a case with PayPal. A charge reversal is also known as a chargeback. When a customer disputes your charge, you can respond to the dispute with evidence that shows the charge is legitimate. Sellers cannot create disputes but can only respond to customer-created disputes.

A buyer would typically raise a dispute with PayPal or the issuing bank. They can do so for 180 days from date of purchase. The buyer and seller have 20 days to negotiate and resolve the issue. If there is no solution disputes can be escalated to a claim and will then be reviewed by PayPal.

Dispute State Machine

• Disputes API allow the seller, marketplaces & authorized 3rd parties to manage disputes outside of paypal.com. • The Disputes API and webhooks allow sellers an end-to-end solution for all the incoming disputes. • Sellers can view, respond to and receive updates on all the PayPal disputes via APIs. • With that level of flexibility, sellers can potentially reduce the cost of servicing and losses.

6.6. Dispute API Appendix

6.6.1. Dispute reasons

Each dispute reason requires certain information for various evidence types:

• MERCHANDISE_OR_SERVICE_NOT_RECEIVED • MERCHANDISE_OR_SERVICE_NOT_AS_DESCRIBED • UNAUTHORISED • CREDIT_NOT_PROCESSED • DUPLICATE_TRANSACTION • INCORRECT_AMOUNT • PAYMENT_BY_OTHER_MEANS • CANCELED_RECURRING_BILLING

6.5. Notifications

Sellers can subscribe to dispute webhooks.

If subscribed to dispute webhooks, sellers are notified about these webhook events

1. CUSTOMER.DISPUTE.CREATED A customer dispute is created. 2. CUSTOMER.DISPUTE.RESOLVED A customer dispute is resolved. 3. CUSTOMER.DISPUTE.UPDATED A customer dispute is updated.

Webhooks are HTTP callbacks that receive notification messages for events. To create a webhook in PayPal, sellers configure a webhook listener and subscribe it to events. A webhook listener is a server that listens at a specific URL for incoming HTTP POST notification messages that are triggered when events occur. PayPal signs each notification message that it delivers to your webhook listener.

Webhooks will (among other) include

• dispute_id • create_time • update_time • disputed_transaction • reason • status • seller_response_due_date • HATEOAS links

6.7. Payouts API

The Payouts API allows you or your sellers -depending on the use case- to send commissions, rebates, rewards, and general disbursements to recipients' PayPal or Venmo accounts.

15000 items). 2. PayPal executes the batch payout asynchronously and sends a webhook for

every single item to inform of their status. 3. (OPTIONAL) PARTNER/MERCHANT NAME checks the payout batch status. 4. (OPTIONAL) PARTNER/MERCHANT NAME checks the item transaction status

for selected item(s).

8.1.1. Introduction

PayPal reports help you manage day-to-day operations by providing transaction-level insight. To begin, create a secure FTP user account on the PayPal site. You can use the following reports with the PayPal Complete Payments:

• Financial Detail Report. Shows a detailed view of the activity on the partner fee transactions. • Financial Summary Report. Provides a summary of the activity on the financial accounts. You can generate this report for a single financial account or for a set of financial accounts, such as the partner fee account or the recoup account. • Marketplace Case Reconciliation report. Shows details for case management processes, including details for cases that were opened or closed during the reporting time frame. Cases include reversals (refunds initiated by PayPal), chargebacks, disputes, and dispute claims. • Payouts Reconciliation report. Shows details for platform activity. Enables you to reconcile the end-to-end money flow and transactions.

PayPal has created a new technical support system, replacing the prior instance which has been retired.

For organizations that permit group ownership of a login and password, we suggest that you may be able to maintain one shared login to the system. Please refer to your organization’s internal security policies if you choose this option. If not possible, you may create an account for each person.

9.4. How to open a ticket with MTS

To ensure a priority level of support you must include the primary email address associated with your seller account in the form when creating the issue, sample below.

If you have more than one account impacted, please use the email address of the most significant account to ensure highest prioritisation.

9.4.1. Logging an MTS ticket

To submit a ticket to MTS, go to https://www.paypal-support.com/ and click on Log in. If you have not created an account after the date of October 23rd, 2018, please see how to do so in section below - Creating a Merchant technical support account.

If you have created an account, please see below for instructions on completing the form.

9.4.2. Completing the form

Choose the Log In button at the top right corner of the page.

You will be prompted with two options - Login with PayPal and Merchant Technical Support Login. Please use the Merchant Technical Support option.

(During account creation, if you are permitted by your organization, you can use one email address to enables multiple people from your organization to share the login to log support requests).

Disclaimer: Please refer to your organization's internal security policies if you choose this option.

Once you log in you will see the following. Click on the Contact Us link to log a ticket.

Complete the following details. Field definitions follow the screenshot below.

9.4.3. Field Definition

Subject & Description: The issue subject & description. Enter the details of the issue / request. Provide as much information as possible, including the research you have conducted on the issue / request.

IMPORTANT - Please report the following information when opening a ticket:

PayPal-debug-id: (see more information below)

Case Collaborators: Enter Enterprise Account Manager and Technical Account Manager's email address to notify both about the issue (separated by semicolon)

Example

Technical Support Identifiers: You can provide log data here (or above in description). Please do not send any API password information – PayPal does not require this.

Upload Attachments: Attachments such as screenshots / source code can be added here.

For a backend issue the PayPal-debug-id is located in the API header response "paypal-debug-id". For a frontend issue the PayPal-debug-id is located in the Web browser Inspect -> Network tab -> Select the URL with the error and look for a PayPal-debug-id in the details (more info at https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/open/ or at https://support.apple.com/en-ie/guide/safari/sfri20948/mac)

Additional details (not mandatory):

Order ID (if applicable)

What percentage of transactions are impacted? Once you have correctly entered the primary email for the account, the issue will be prioritised, so you can select an accurate % of transactions impacted without lowering the priority of the issue.

What is the urgency of the request? Once you have correctly entered the primary email for the account, the urgency of issue will be prioritised, so you can select an accurate level of urgency without lowering the priority of the issue.

Select Product you are contacting us about: Select from the dropdown (Express Checkout is considered 'PayPal Checkout').

Environment: Production (Live) or Sandbox. Both are treated with required priority.
